events.title: WTI Crude Back Above $100/bbl on Escalating Middle East Conflict
events.summary: Failed ceasefire negotiations caused WTI crude to end the week above $100 per barrel. This is the dominant macro theme driving cross-asset repricing: higher inflation expectations, wider credit spreads, and rising Treasury yields.
events.why_now: Ceasefire optimism faded during the week ending March 27, 2026, pushing oil prices sharply higher. Markets are now repricing inflation risk and growth expectations heading into Q2.
events.market_relevance: Oil above $100 is a stagflationary signal. It raises input costs across the economy, compresses consumer discretionary spending, widens credit spreads (high yield widened 46 bps vs Treasuries in one week), and complicates the Fed's rate-cut path. Energy producers benefit while airlines, chemicals, and consumer-facing sectors face margin pressure.

events.title: 10-Year Treasury Yield Rises to 4.43%, Breaching Nuveen's Forecast Range
events.summary: The 10-year Treasury yield rose 5 bps to 4.43%, now above Nuveen's projected 4.00%-4.25% year-end range. The 30-year yield reached 4.97%. The move reflects oil-driven inflation repricing rather than growth optimism.
events.why_now: Energy-driven inflation expectations are pushing yields higher at a time when the Fed is on pause. The yield curve steepened with 30-year yields near 5%, signaling term premium expansion.
events.market_relevance: Higher long-end yields directly raise mortgage rates, increase corporate borrowing costs, compress equity valuations (especially growth/duration-sensitive stocks), and pressure leveraged real estate. The 30-year near 5% is a psychologically important level for housing affordability.

events.title: Fed Rate Cut Timeline Pushed Back — Second Cut May Slip to 2027
events.summary: Nuveen now expects only two 25-bp Fed rate cuts in 2026, with near-term inflationary pressures from oil potentially pushing the second cut into 2027. The March FOMC held rates steady, consistent with J.P. Morgan's coverage of the statement.
events.why_now: Oil-driven inflation is extending the Fed's pause. Markets had been pricing in more accommodative policy; the shift toward fewer and later cuts reprices rate-sensitive assets.
events.market_relevance: A delayed easing cycle keeps financial conditions tighter for longer, pressuring leveraged borrowers, floating-rate debt holders, and rate-sensitive sectors. It also delays mortgage rate relief for housing.

events.title: High Yield and Preferred Spreads Widen Sharply on Stagflation Fears
events.summary: High yield corporates underperformed Treasuries by 46 bps and preferreds by 45 bps in a single week. Emerging market debt also widened by 23 bps. The spread widening reflects dual concerns: higher input costs squeezing margins and slower growth.
events.why_now: The combination of $100+ oil and rising yields is a classic stagflationary setup that hits lower-quality credit hardest. This widening occurred rapidly, suggesting potential for further dislocation if oil stays elevated.
events.market_relevance: Wider high yield spreads raise the cost of capital for leveraged issuers, increase default risk pricing, and can trigger forced selling in credit ETFs and CLOs. Preferred spread widening hits bank capital instruments and income-oriented investors.

events.title: Municipal Bonds Down 2.72% Month-to-Date, Underperforming Treasuries
events.summary: Investment grade munis returned -0.81% for the week and -2.72% month-to-date, significantly underperforming Treasuries (-2.34% MTD). The muni yield of 3.83% with 6.81 years duration suggests tax-exempt investors are facing notable losses.
events.why_now: Rising rates combined with potential supply pressures and tax-loss selling dynamics are hitting munis harder than taxable bonds. This is unusual and may signal liquidity stress or repositioning ahead of Q2.
events.market_relevance: Muni underperformance affects state and local government borrowing costs, infrastructure project financing, and the wealth of high-net-worth investors who hold munis for tax efficiency. It may also signal stress in muni-focused funds.

events.title: Federal Reserve Publishes Final 2026 Bank Stress Test Scenarios
events.summary: The Fed released final 2026 stress test scenarios incorporating updated data through year-end 2025. The severely adverse scenario retains its narrative and severity from the proposal, with updated jump-off values. The global market shock component was also adjusted.
events.why_now: Final scenarios set the parameters for how much capital large banks must hold. Banks are now running models against these scenarios, which will determine capital return capacity (buybacks, dividends) announced later in 2026.
events.market_relevance: Stress test results directly determine bank capital buffers and shareholder return programs. If the severely adverse scenario is harsh (especially on CRE losses), banks with large commercial real estate exposures may face higher capital requirements, limiting buybacks.

events.title: Key Data Week Ahead: March PMIs, JOLTS, and China Manufacturing Data
events.summary: Markets face March PMI readings for the U.S. and China, plus the February JOLTS report. These will provide the first hard signals on how the Middle East conflict and elevated oil prices are affecting business sentiment and labor demand.
events.why_now: With oil above $100 and spreads widening, these data releases will either confirm or challenge the stagflation narrative. A weak PMI combined with still-tight labor data would reinforce the case for extended Fed pause.
events.market_relevance: PMI readings below 50 would signal manufacturing contraction and accelerate the growth scare. JOLTS data showing declining openings would suggest labor market cooling, potentially offsetting inflation concerns and shifting the Fed calculus. China PMI affects global commodity demand and supply chain pricing.
